titleStmt.title: Letter from Ernest Blythe to George Gavan Duffy, 12 May 1916.
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Ernest Blythe

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Ernest Blythe (1889-1975) to George Gavan Duffy (1882-1951). Only the first page of the letter has survived. Blythe has been detained in Brixton Prison, England, and he has seven days to make representations. He asks Duffy to advise and assist him. George Gavan Duffy was an Irish politician, barrister and judge. He unsuccessfully defended Roger Casement at his trial for high treason after the Easter Rising. Earnest Blythe was an organiser for the Irish Volunteers. He had been arrested under the Defence of the Realm Act. He was released from prison in England before the Easter Rising but was rearrested there during Easter week 1916.

msIdentifier.collection: Bureau of Military History Contemporary Documents, George Gavan Duffy Collection, BMH CD/45/3/11

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
In replying to this letter, please write on the envelope:- Number 313 Name Blythe E Brixton Prison 12 th May 1916 Dear Mr. Gavin Duffy, I was
interned here on Wednesday
by order of the Home Secretary.
I am allowed, within seven
days, to make representations
against the provisions of the
order and I think it better
to have your advice and
assistance in doing so. As
